video_title: Landrover Air suspension Fault, Explained, Fixed
transcript: [Music] hi everybody and welcome back to a new episode of diagnos Dan today we're working on a 2013 Land Rover Discovery and this vehicle has got an air suspension problem of course now as far as I know they already replaced the compressor they replaced some other parts but still the vehicle is coming up with a message in the dask that the air suspension is not happy so finally they asked us to take a look so let's see if we can diagnose this one [Music] together according to the customer the air suspension works just fine the car raises the car lowers and it doesn't drop overnight the only problem he experiences is that every time he starts the vehicle a m Mage pops up in the dash that there is a fault in the air suspension system now in The Next Step let's confirm that customer complaint so let's start up the vehicle and let's confirm the customer's complaint let's take a look at that instrument cluster and I hear the compressor kick in right now it says maintenance required and the compressor kicks in the compressor stops and we've got the message right there now I know you all speak Dutch but for the ones who don't right there it says air suspension defect or air suspension male function so that's customer complaint confirmed now according to the customer apart from that message the air suspension works just fine now let's confirm that let's lower the vehicle the vehicle lowered just fine so let's try and raise the vehicle now just like the customer said the car does lower and it does rise but I must say the raisin goes very very slow now that could indicate a weak pump but I've been told the pump has already been replaced the compressor on this vehicle has been replaced very recently and the old unit is in the back of the vehicle so let's take a look and this is the compressor that has been replaced now it is an a we compressor amk is an original supplier but to be honest this compressor doesn't look too old to me it's certainly not the unit the car left the factory with uh 12 years ago now let's take a look if there's a date on there and indeed there is a date it says 28622 so that means this compressor is only 3 years old so not that old so I wonder if that compressor was bad in the first place in the next step let's see what fault Cates are stored in the air suspension system but before we do that let's hook up the car through a battery charger so we don't have to worry about the battery's voltage got the battery hooked up to a charger so we don't have to worry about the battery voltage while we're diagnosing the vehicle now in The Next Step let's read the fault codes I've read theault codes in the rlm which is the right level control module and there's only one fault code stored the c1a 1864 pressure increases to Rapid when filling the reservoir now I don't work on a lot of Land Rover so I'm no expert but this is definitely a first for me um what is more common is that it's not filling fast enough and that would indicate a weak compressor but obviously the compressor on this vehicle has already been replaced now what happens every time I start up the vehicle I hear the compressor kick in the compressor stays on for a few seconds and it then turns off now at the same moment it turns off I'm getting the message in the instrument cluster that we've got a fault in the air suspension system now what I did is I pulled up the data pit for the pressure in in the air suspension system and I will record my screen and put it up there for you guys to see but in the next step I'm going to start the vehicle and we're going to take a look at what's happening with that pressure so let's start up the vehicle there we go and the compressor kicks in let's see what happens now let's take a closer look at that screen recording we can see that the gallery pressure starts off around four bars and when the compressor kicks in we can see that the pressure Rises rapidly to over 16 bars and at that point the control unit says enough is enough and it shuts down the compressor and it sets a fault code that the reservoir is filling too rapidly now I think it looks at the pressure and it looks at that and thinks this is rising too rapidly and it then shuts down that compressor now what could cause the pressure in the gallery to rise that quickly most of the components for the air suspension system are located somewhere underneath the vehicle right here underneath that plastic cover so in the next step I want to remove that wheel jack up the vehicle and remove that plastic cover so we can take a closer look at some of the components I jacked up the vehicle remove the wheel and remove this plastic cover that covers the compressor now indeed we can see that that compressor is is pretty much brand new now in front of that compressor we've got a valve block and in that valve block there is a pressure sensor that we were looking at in the data pit now I want to take a closer look at this valve block which has got a solenoid and a pressure sensor but it's quite high up there so I want to see if I can get it to drop down a little bit now I was able to pull down this valve block a little bit so we can have a closer look now on this valve block there are four air connections one two three and one at the top and that's four now we also have got a pressure sensor right there and that is the pressure sensor we were looking at earlier in the data pin now we also got a connector over there that controls a solenoid now this line right here runs directly to the compressor right there so this is where the compressed air makes it into the valve block now you can see that there is a little Gap gallery now inside that Gallery is the pressure sensor so when the pressure in that Gallery Rises it will be registered by this pressure sensor now there are three directions for the air to go first of all it can go to the rear axle now at the rear axle there's another valve block and that valve block is normally closed unless you want to raise the rear of the vehicle so when you want to fill the airbags now there is another way for the air to go and that is into this line now that line runs to the front axle now at the front axle again there's another valve block which is normally closed unless you want to raise the vehicle now there is a third direction for the air to go and that is into this line and that line runs to a reservoir now this is a reservoir that's underneath the vehicle and in this Reservoir the vehicle can store compressed air which is can use to rapidly raise the vehicle when needed now in order for the air to flow into the reservoir this solenoid needs to open when the control unit is trying to fill the reservoir it has to open this valve now when this valve doesn't open the air has got nowhere to go it can't go towards the rear axle Because unless we're trying to raise the vehicle the solenoid or the valve block at the rear axle is normally closed now same goes for the line towards the front axle that is also normally closed now when this valve doesn't open the air can also not flow into this Reservoir so when the compressor kicks in and the air has got nowhere to go what happens is that the air or the air pressure inside this little Gallery will rise very fast and that's exactly what we were seeing in that graph on the data pit on the scan tool now when this valve does open the control unit would expect the pressure to rise very slowly because it takes quite a while to fill this Reservoir now since the pressure is rising very rapidly it sets a fault code for the reservoir filling too [Music] fast hi everybody today in the workshop we've got a 2016 Audi Q7 rron it's hybrid with in this case a 3 l TDI engine now this specific vehicle comes up with an add blue warning in the instent panel and a quick scan of the vehicle shows us that we've got multiple fault codes stored for the reductant pump the reductant pump or at Blue Pump is located inside the at blue tank and we have seen that many workshops will replace the at blue tank for this exact same fault code now the at blue tank is not only very expensive in most cases it will also not fix the issue a quick search in ddsb learns us that these vehicles suffer from a bad wiring harness running up to the ad blue tank we can see that in some cases the wires are even completely broken now before ordering that very expensive at blue tank let's quickly have a look underneath this vehicle the at blue tank on this vehicle is located underneath this panel behind the rear right wheel now when we pull down this panel a little bit we can see green crusties on that panel now you can see that that panel is very close to that wiring harness and when it starts vibrating it starts cutting into that harness now you can already see some green crusties on that wiring harness and I bet when we're going to pull that that will snap very easily in a modern Workshop we must work as efficient as possible to survive but also to stay competitive now without the right knowledge or uh experience this could have turned into very expensive and timec consuming job and you might not have fixed the vehicle now with the right information this was walk in the park it took me less than 3 minutes now if you're also interested in working cost efficient reducing stress and highly reduce the number of comebacks then check out the link in the description see you next time [Music] guys now they're actually could be multiple reasons for the pressure inside this gallery to rise too quickly we could have a compressor that is delivering too much pressure which in this case I think is unlikely because they had exactly the same problem with the old compressor it could be that the pressure sensor is reading a wrong value it could be that the solenoid is not being controlled it could also be that the solenoid is stuck or it could be a clogged line that could be multiple reasons now in the next step I want to start out by checking if the control unit is actually trying to control or trying to open this valve The Next Step I want to find out if the control unit is able to control that valve so I hooked up my test light on that connector and with the help of a scan to in The Next Step we're going to command that solenoid to open now if the test light lights up that would indicate that the control unit is able to control that valve the test light is connected to the connector of that Reservoir valve now I tape the test light on the window so it's easy for you guys to see what's happening now let's open that valve and let's set it to let's say 50% there we go let's activate it and as you can see the test light is lighting up so this means there's no problem with the control side of that solenoid now that we know that the control unit is able to control that valve I reconnected that connector and we're going to activate that valve again with the scan tool now if it's working we should hear it clicking so let's actuate it there we go now it's on right now but as you can hear it doesn't do anything at all what I did next is I disconnected that connector from that Reservoir valve again I cleared the fault codes and read the fault codes again and immediately a fault code comes back for that Reservoir valve now what does that tell us it tells us that the control unit is able to see that that connector is disconnected now that proves that the wiring is fine now we already knew that because with the test light we saw that the control unit was able to control that solenoid now it tells us something else as well if there was a break in the coil of that valve it would be exactly the same as disconnecting that connector so this proves that there's nothing electrically wrong with that valve but it's probably mechanically stuck right now I'm pretty sure that that valve is mechanically stuck so on the next step I'm going to remove it from the vehicle once it is removed um we're going to see if we can maybe take it apart I'm not sure but hopefully we will find the reason of why that valve is sticking I removed the valve block from the vehicle and what immediately stands out to me is that this valve block looks pretty new to me now I don't know the full history of this vehicle but I can definitely tell this valve block has been replaced at some point now when we take a closer look we can clearly see that these three ports are all connected to the same Gallery the only one that's separated by this valve is the one over here and that's the one that runs to the reservoir so if the compressor wants to fill the reservoir this valve has to open now this also goes the other way around if the control unit wants to quickly raise the vehicle and use the stored air in the reservoir this valve also has to open to allow that compressed air to move towards the front or rear axle now this all makes sense now because when we were trying to raise the vehicle that went very slowly that means that the only thing raising the vehicle was the compressor and it was not being assisted by the stored air in the reservoir now we already kind of new but just to be sure and to have a known good for future reference I'm measuring the electrical side of the solenoid now we're reading about 2.5 2.6 ohm which is perfectly fine just like expected now in the top of this valve there are three screws now I'm going to remove it and hopefully we can take it apart to see what's going on [Music] I separated the valve block into the mechanical part and the electrical part which we know is fine now when we take a look at the mechanical part there is a little piston which we can pull out and let me see if there's something in there now if we twist this and the other way this comes apart and then we should be able to see the part that actually moves when the solenoid is activated and that's this part and it actually does move I inspected all the parts to the best of my ability and actually cannot find a reason why this valve shouldn't work now I don't see any debris I don't see any witness marks the only thing that I do notice is that there little um piston in this cylinder is has quite a lot of room so it's quite wobbly so if it's on an angle it's hard to push in now in the next step I want to clean everything I want to blow this out with compressed air and I'm going to assemble it again and see if it then works I cleaned it all out and put it all back together now in the next step I want to connect that connector again and activate this valve with the scan tool and hopefully it will do its thing I connected that connector to the valve block for that solenoid now let's activate it again with the scan tool there we go and I don't know if you guys can hear that but let me put the mic up to that valve block it looks like it is working again the valve block is working again but the fact I didn't find anything wrong with it I was hoping I would find something jammed in there and that's got me worried a little bit because that makes this valve unreliable it could happen again because we didn't really find something that was causing the issue now what I think is that this part is poor quality if you just look at it it looks cheap if you also look at the print it's got a cheap print on there and the fact that the Piston has got a lot of room and when it gets in an angle it gets stuck um made me call the customer and we decided to replace it with an original one I already called the dealership theyve got one on stock so I'll jump in my car uh get one hopefully I'm back in an hour I will install it and hopefully the car will be fixed after that I installed the new valve block and this is the old one well old one I actually don't think it's that old now everything is back together so in theory everything should work again as intended but again that's in theory now let's go inside the car and find out we're inside the car let's start it up there we go and let's wait for a little bit now that is maintainance required again I will notify the customer and ask him what he wants with that and it looks like so far so good now in the next step I want to lower and raise the vehicle again and hopefully that will go a little bit smoother and faster than it did before so let's lower the vehicle I put my toolbox next to the vehicle for reference now let's lower it and it lowers very smoothly now let's raise it again and as you can see it raises instantly now let's do that one more time lower it and raise it absolutely fantastic now let's see what it looks like from inside the vehicle let's start it up now let's uh lower the vehicle and as you can see it lowers instantly now let's raise the vehicle and it also comes up instantly and as you can see nothing on the dash so it looks like we've got to fix now once again Quality Parts are really really important and when something has recently replaced don't automatically rule it out as being the cause of your fault if you look at a part and you think hey that has been recently replaced so that can't be the cause you might end up going down the wrong path and replace parts that didn't need replacing in the first place now I really hope you enjoyed this video I sure did and for a change I actually liked working on a Land Rover this time now if you did please subscribe to my channel and when you hit the little bell you will get a notification each time I upload a new video and remember diagnose then fix it again see you next time guys far as I know they already rep report fault in the air suspension system so in the N the car is the car low now that we've learned that the control unit is able to now what does this what does that tell us about our wire Integrity that means a wi wire um we can clearly see that these three p 3 p 3 is that this valve block looks pretty new to me now I don't know that I installed the new valve block I didn't want to bother you with that this I reassembled everything the car is ready to go so let's go in but everything is back together put the wheel back in put the ground [Music]
